Gross margin profile

Low-to-mid 20s

Low 20s

Above, I’ve compared the company’s value in October 2025 against today, mid-February 2026. What stands out to me is that the valuation now represents a stock that is priced as if it has a lot to prove, which is true.

It is cheaper today, but it is not compelling just yet. We’ve seen the gross margin bottom out, and I need it to get back to the upper mid-20s range and stay there for a few quarters first. That will only happen if the topline grows faster than we’ve seen so far in H1 FY26, and I’m unwilling to stick my neck out and say that said growth is already on the horizon.

I don’t consider valuation as a headwind at this point. Conversely, it also isn’t a tailwind on its own just because the stock is cheap. In my opinion, it is fairly priced for a business that is trying to achieve some level of earnings durability.

What Would Trigger a Rating Upgrade for Me

Management is confident that the Q2 performance was a one-off. I’m not too convinced about that, but we’ll have to wait and see. Again, the main thing I’m watching over the next few quarters is consistency with the numbers.

Gross margins need to get back to Q1’s levels or even higher, and they ideally should stay there for several consecutive quarters. Given that management is devoting 25% more money to R&D in 2026 than they did in calendar 2025, I don’t see how that will happen without some major improvements in the product mix and topline.

Operating leverage also needs to keep trending in the right direction. Sustaining bigger losses is intolerable at this point, given all the divestments and renewed focus on core business areas that management is preaching. I don’t think it is sustainable for AOSL’s cost structure to keep breaking down whenever it has to deal with softer demand and volume. Revenue should increase moving forward, and I expect those operating losses to be even narrower when that happens.

Final Thoughts

All things considered, AOSL is a better-run company today than it was a year ago, but it still needs to work on several things to build investor confidence in its fundamentals and trajectory. I don’t believe that the company’s near-term financial profile supports a re-rating for the stock price, so a Hold is the reasonable option at this point.

data.title: United Natural Foods: Staying Patient
data.description:
Shares of food distributor United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI) have been somewhat volatile a quarter after rallying in early October 2025 due to a much stronger-than-anticipated FY26 outlook due to efficiency gains. That outlook was maintained in December 2025—when the company reported 1QFY26 financials in December—and followed by wholly achievable FY28 objectives revealed at its Investor Day. With net leverage projected to fall from 4.2 in 1QFY25 to sub 2.0 by YEFY27 while trading at approximately 14.4 times FY27E EPS with tepid Street support, the recent insider buying in UNFI merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

United Natural Foods, Inc. is a Providence, Rhode Island-based distributor of ~230,000 grocery and non-food products from nearly 10,000 suppliers to all 50 states and Canada. The company, known as UNFI, services its 30,000+ customer locations from 49 distribution centers and warehouses comprising ~28 million square feet. With roots dating back to 1976, UNFI was formed through the merger of regional distributors Mountain People’s Warehouse and Cornucopia Natural Foods, becoming the first nationwide natural food distributor in 1996. It went public that same year, raising net proceeds of $35.5 million at $6.75 per share, after giving effect to a 2-for-1 stock split in 2004. Its stock trades around $40 a share, translating to a market cap just over $2.4 billion.

The company operates on a 52- or 53-week fiscal year (FY) ending the Saturday closest to July 31st. For the avoidance of doubt, the 52-week period ending August 2, 2025, is FY25.

Operating Segments

The company views the performance of its operations through three business segments: Natural; Conventional; and Retail.

Approximately half of UNFI’s top line comes from Natural, which consists of natural, organic, and specialty grocery and non-food products and services, including UNFI’s natural and organic brands and manufacturing operation. Servicing a $60 billion industry growing at mid-single digits that includes its largest customer (Amazon’s (AMZN) Whole Foods), it generated FY25 Adj. EBITDA of $442 million on net sales of $15.96 billion versus Adj. EBITDA of $350 million on net sales of $14.87 billion in FY24, representing improvements of 26% and 7%, respectively.

Conventional consists of what the company deems “conventional” (processed) grocery and non-food products. Servicing a stagnate $30 billion industry, the segment generated FY25 Adj. EBITDA of $174 million on net sales of $13.48 billion versus Adj. EBITDA of $219 million on net sales of $13.68 billion, representing declines of 21% and 1%, respectively.

Retail consists of UNFI’s 75 Cub Foods and Shoppers retail grocery stores, which offer both their own private label products as well as national and local brands. The unit provided FY25 Adj. EBITDA of $6 million on net sales of $2.34 billion versus Adj. EBITDA of $8 million on net sales of $2.44 billion in FY24, representing declines of 25% and 4%, respectively.

Putting it all together with corporate overhead, UNFI generated FY25 earnings of $0.71 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $552 million on net sales of $31.78 billion versus $0.14 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $518 million on net sales of $30.98 billion in FY24, representing gains of 407%, 7%, and 3%, respectively—9% and 5% at the Adj. EBITDA and net sales lines if FY24’s 53rd week is excluded.

Share Price Performance

Coming out of a poor half-decade triggered by the termination of its contract with Albertson’s (ACI) in 2015, further exacerbated by a regretful $2.9 billion acquisition of SuperValu stores in 2018—and later by pandemic uncertainty—UNFI's stock deteriorated from an all-time high of $83.91 per share to a two-decade low of $5.00 per share in March 2020. Emerging from the pandemic, the company enjoyed high (mid-14%) gross profits from food inflation (that peaked at 10.4% in 2022), generating a post-pandemic peak Adj. EBITDA of $829 million in FY22. Shares of UNFI reflected this dynamic, cresting at a six-year high of $57.89 in late 2021. However, as food inflation returned to the mid-2s, gross margins contracted 90 to 100 basis points over the ensuing two fiscal years (from mid-14% to mid-13%), while operating expense inflation continued, shrinking Adj. EBITDA to a six-year low of $518 million in FY24. Reflecting these headwinds, shares of UNFI fell 85% to $8.58 in April 2024, showcasing somewhat significant volatility for an otherwise dull, low-margin food distributor.

At that juncture, the company onboarded a new CFO, Giorgio Tarditi from General Electric (GE), joining CEO Sandy Douglas, who was appointed to his role in 2021 after a stint as the CEO of Staples. Shortly thereafter, UNFI entered into an eight-year extension to its distribution partnership with Whole Foods, and in January 2025, it realigned its commercial wholesale organization into the aforementioned natural and conventional segments, later embarking on a distribution optimization plan known as ‘lean management,’ which piggybacked on its $600 million investment in digitization that started in 2023—all in an effort to improve gross and operating margins. Aided by positive industry volume trends, shares of UNFI got off the mat and traded between $20 and $35 per share until its 4QFY25 financial report in late September 2025, in which it provided FY26 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $1.90 versus a then consensus of $1.49, providing evidence that its efficiency initiatives were gaining traction while igniting a seven-day, 34% rally to $42.51 per share.

1QFY26 Financial Report & FY26 Guidance

Since that time, shares of UNFI have more or less round-tripped. That said, their 24% decline was not attributable to the company’s 1QFY26 financial report announced on December 2, 2025, when it posted non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 per share and Adj. EBITDA of $167 million on net sales of $7.84 billion versus $0.16 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $134 million on net sales of $7.87 billion in 1QFY25, reflecting improvements of 250% and 25% on a 0.4% decrease in net sales, the latter of which was a function of a 12% decline in conventional sales due to UNFI closing its Allentown, PA distribution center, offset by an 11% increase in its natural segment. Gross profit improved 20 basis points year-over-year to 13.4% as management’s efforts to decrease shrink (unaccounted loss of inventory due to theft, fraud, waste, inefficient processes, etc.) took increasing effect.

Furthermore, lean management practices were instituted at six additional distribution centers during the quarter, bringing the total to 34.

Management reiterated its FY26 guidance that sparked the late-September/early-October rally, maintaining earnings of $1.90 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $665 million on net sales of $31.8 billion, all based on range midpoints. During its Investor Day the following week, the company laid out its FY28 goals, including free cash flow of $300 million and Adj. EBITDA of $800 million on revenue of $33 billion, spurred mainly through efficiency gains. With UNFI’s strong showing at the bottom line, which beat expectations by $0.16, further suggesting that its initiatives were gaining significant momentum, the market rallied its stock 5% to $35.95 per share in the subsequent trading session, but that proved to be their highest close for the balance of 2025. Its shares are currently down 24% from their 2025 closing high set in October.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

In addition to the improvement at the earnings line, the company lowered its year-over-year net debt from $2.23 billion to $1.90 billion on November 1, 2025. Also aided by improvement in Adj. EBITDA, net leverage fell a full turn year-over-year to 3.2, with management eyeing 2.5 or below by YEFY26 and sub-2.0 by YEFY27. As for returning capital to shareholders, UNFI is focused on debt reduction. As such, it does not pay a dividend, and although it has a share repurchase authorization with $138 million remaining, it did not buy back any stock in FY25 or 1Q26.

Despite evidence of improvement at its bottom line, the company does not enjoy strong support from the Street, as can be seen above. That said, the last rating change was to the positive when BMO Capital upgraded UNFI from a hold to a buy in September 2025. On average, sell-side analysts expect UNFI to earn $2.18 per share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $31.9 billion in FY26, followed by $2.77 per share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $32.6 billion in FY27.

Board member James Pappas, representing the interests of JCP Investment Management, does not agree with Street consensus, purchasing 17,000 shares at an average price of $33.71 on January 2, 2026.

Verdict:

Although the gains from its optimization efforts appear small—20 basis points here or there—in a business with slim margins and ~$32 billion in sales, the impact on UNFI’s bottom line is tangible. The bet here is that they are also lasting, with the company’s FY28 goals very much attainable. That said, with a stated ‘goal’ for an FY28 Adj. EBITDA margin of 2.4%, UNFI’s stock is not a screaming value at just over 14 times FY27E non-GAAP EPS.

One month ago, UNFI was recommended as a small, covered call trade exclusively to Insider Forum members. The stock was trading at $34.00 a share at that time and has advanced nearly 20% since then. I would wait for the stock to fall back into the mid-$30s before I would reiterate that recommendation.
